questions.text: What variable holds the current item in an Ansible loop?

options.text: {{ current }}
{{ loop.item }}
{{ item }}
{{ each }}

questions.explanation: Within a loop, the current iteration's value is available as {{ item }}. For dict items, access properties with {{ item.key }}. The loop variable also provides loop.index (1-based), loop.index0 (0-based), loop.first, and loop.last.

questions.text: Which keyword is preferred over 'with_items' in modern Ansible?

options.text: foreach
loop
iterate
each

questions.explanation: The 'loop' keyword is the modern replacement for with_items in Ansible 2.5+. It's more consistent and composable with filters. Legacy with_* keywords still work but 'loop' with filters is preferred: loop: "{{ items | flatten(levels=1) }}".

questions.text: What does the 'rescue' section in a block do?

options.text: Runs tasks before the block starts
Runs tasks only if the block succeeds
Runs tasks if any task in the block fails
Rolls back changes made in the block

questions.explanation: The rescue section runs if any task in the block fails, similar to a try/catch pattern. It lets you handle failures gracefully — send alerts, clean up, or attempt recovery. After rescue completes successfully, the play continues (the failure is cleared).

questions.text: What does 'always:' in a block guarantee?

options.text: Tasks run with the highest privilege
Tasks run regardless of whether block or rescue succeeded or failed
Tasks run before the block's tasks
Tasks run only in check mode

questions.explanation: The always section runs regardless of the block's outcome — whether it succeeded, failed, or rescue ran. This makes it ideal for cleanup operations, sending status notifications, or ensuring critical services are in a known state.

options.text: Skips the task if it would fail
Allows the play to continue even if the task fails
Suppresses all output from the task
Retries the task up to 3 times before marking as failed

questions.explanation: ignore_errors: true causes Ansible to continue the play even if a task fails. The task still shows as 'failed' in output and is recorded in the stats, but the play continues. Use sparingly — it's better to handle expected failures with failed_when or block/rescue.

questions.text: How do you add a pause of 2 seconds between loop iterations?

options.text: loop_pause: 2
loop_control: delay: 2
loop_control: pause: 2
with_delay: 2

questions.explanation: loop_control with the pause parameter adds a delay between iterations: loop_control: pause: 2. This is useful for rate limiting API calls or avoiding overwhelming a service during rolling updates.

questions.text: What does 'changed_when: false' do on a task?

options.text: Prevents the task from making any changes
Marks the task as never 'changed', even if the module reports changed
Skips the task if nothing would change
Disables handler notifications for this task

questions.explanation: changed_when: false forces a task to always report 'ok' (not 'changed'), even if the underlying command modified something. This is useful for command/shell tasks that you know are read-only but the module can't determine this automatically.

questions.text: Which combination of keywords implements retry with polling?

options.text: retry: 5 and sleep: 3
until:, retries:, and delay:
wait_for: and timeout:
poll: and async:

questions.explanation: The until/retries/delay combination retries a task until a condition is met. Example: register: result until: result.status == 200 retries: 10 delay: 5. This polls every 5 seconds for up to 10 attempts. until uses the registered variable from the previous attempt.

citations.label: Retrying a task until a condition is met

questions.text: How do you loop over a dictionary's key-value pairs in Ansible?

options.text: loop: "{{ mydict }}" and use item.key, item.value
loop: "{{ mydict | dict2items }}" and use item.key, item.value
with_dict: mydict and use item.key, item.value
loop: mydict.items()

questions.explanation: To loop over a dictionary, use the dict2items filter: loop: "{{ mydict | dict2items }}". Each item will have item.key and item.value. The legacy with_dict: also works but loop with dict2items is the modern approach.

questions.text: What does 'any_errors_fatal: true' do at the play level?

options.text: Makes all errors show full stack traces
Stops the entire play if any host fails, not just the failing host
Prevents ignore_errors from being used
Enables strict YAML mode

questions.explanation: any_errors_fatal: true causes Ansible to stop the entire play across all hosts if any single host fails a task. By default, a failed task only removes that host from the play while others continue. This is useful for deployment scenarios where partial completion is dangerous.
